Comparing a defense card's OPS against the attack pack would mis-flag the entire defensive suite as 'weak'. The honest read is: defense cards must be judged on cushion/survival contribution (where they are saturated at ~1.0) and on defense-vs-defense OPS, not against attacks. No defense card in scope shows an off-curve cushion deficit. This caveat also explains why the headline winRate/cushion fields in card-appearance barely discriminate physical cards (fixed-order decks nearly all win) — TTK swap-tests, not those saturated fields, are the discriminating signal used above.
finding.proposed_change: No card change. Methodological note for the synthesizer: do not treat low-OPS defense cards as off-curve-weak; off-curve detection for physical cards should rest on (a) attack-vs-attack peakOPS pack position and (b) marginal TTK/cushion in swap tests, both of which were used for the weak findings above.

verdict.target: physical defense cards (T1-T3, category=defense, stamina) — low peakOPS is by design, not off-curve
verdict.title: Defense-category cards' low peakOPS must NOT be read as off-curve-weak (C3: armor/heal/stun are intentionally race-losing)
verdict.reason: CONFIRMED as a valid methodology caveat. Verified directly against card-appearance.json and tier-ops-perf.json; no swap-test needed because the finding proposes NO card change — it is about how to interpret OPS for an entire category, which I checked against the data. (1) peakOPS is isolated DAMAGE-per-second, so armor/heal/stun cards score low by construction — exactly what WORKFLOW-BRIEF C3 mandates ('armor/heal/stun are INTENTIONALLY race-losing'). (2) The attack-vs-defense OPS gap is large and consistent at every tier (recomputed medians: T1 attack 24.17 vs defense 6; T2 attack 25.42 vs defense 7; T3 attack 28.92 vs defense 8.67), so comparing defense OPS against attack OPS would mis-flag the whole defensive suite as weak. (3) Defense cards are saturated on their actual charter (survival): meanNormalCushion 0.878-1.0 for the 19 defense cards with nDecks>=5, NONE below 0.85, headline normal winRate ~1.0 and bossWin 1.0 across the board — no off-curve cushion deficit exists. (4) Named cards verified via cards-index: t1-defense=Guard (OPS 6, cush 0.935), t3-defense-defense-defense=Aegis of Returning Wrath (OPS 14, cush 1.0), t2-counter-earth=Thornwall (OPS 11.7, cush 0.999), t2-defense-earth=Bramble Bulwark (OPS 10, cush 1.0) — all as cited. (5) Static-confirmed bugs do not duplicate this caveat. The proposed_change (no card edit; synthesizer note to judge defense on cushion/defense-vs-defense OPS, not against attacks) is fully C1-C5 compliant. Severity correctly LOW (it is a guardrail against false positives, not a balance defect). Minor accuracy note: the finding quotes prior-audit medians T2 15.57 / T3 15.33; my fresh recompute from card-appearance gives T2 16 / T3 16.5 — slightly different source/rounding but directionally identical and the conclusion is unchanged.
verdict.sim_evidence: No swap-test run (finding proposes no card change). Direct data recheck: card-appearance OPS by tier x category — T1 attack median 24.17 (n=3) vs defense 6 (n=1); T2 attack 25.42 (n=12) vs defense 7 (n=9); T3 attack 28.92 (n=51) vs defense 8.67 (n=46). Critically, removing defense cards restores T3>T2: T3 median jumps from 16.5 (all, n=120) to 25.42 (non-defense, n=74), while T2 goes 16 (all, n=36) to 18.75 (non-defense, n=27) — proving the pooled-median 'T3 not higher than T2' anomaly is an artifact of T3 carrying 46 race-losing defense cards vs T2's 9. Defense cushion saturation: 19 defense cards with nDecks>=5 span cushion 0.878-1.0, zero below 0.85; e.g. Guard 0.935, Aegis 1.0, Thornwall 0.999, Bramble Bulwark 1.0, Stoneward Reprisal 1.0 — all with normal winRate 1.0 and bossWin 1.0.

finding.title: Quench Lance (T3 fire) under-performs the T2 fire detonator Pyre on every normal — its build-around INT payoff is practically un-feedable
finding.evidence: Swap-test spec tests/audit/mage-offcurve-matchups.json (6 repeats). A fire deck anchored by Quench Lance (T3) closes SLOWER than the identical deck anchored by Pyre (T2) on all three normals: vs skeleton TTK 11000ms (T3) vs 8900ms (T2, Pyre); vs werewolf 12000 vs 8900; vs depths_horror 9000 vs 6750 — i.e. the T3 is 24-35% slower and runs LOWER cushion (skeleton 0.802 T3 vs 0.851 T2; werewolf 0.711 vs 0.769). isolated peakOPS: Quench Lance 8.33 vs Pyre 36.43 vs Flame Dart 57.5 (tier-ops-perf median T3 should EXCEED T2). Root cause confirmed in code: cards.json t3-fire-fire-water effects = [dot 2 burn (int per3); aura event_counter {event:'stack_consumed', filter:{stack:'burn', min_amount:10}, threshold 1} -> stat_gain int]. The card itself only applies 2 burn and has NO consume effect; its INT payoff needs ANOTHER card to emit a stack_consumed event of 10+ burn. Per static-confirmed.json (Pyre-keyword burn detonators), the common burn detonators (Cinderlance/Cinder Sprint/Venom Detonation) NEVER emit stack_consumed (the Pyre auto-consume zeroes burn before the explicit consume runs), so only Supernova/Tremor Detonate can ever feed Quench Lance — and burn's per-tick soft-cap 8+floor((n-8)/2) plus chunk-cap make a single 10+ consume rare for a low-INT (~3-4 at loop10) mage. The build-around payoff is effectively dead in most fire decks.
finding.diagnosis: T3 charter requires T3 to out-ceiling any T2 of the same element/role (~+15-25% assembled), fixing violations by BUFFING the T3. Quench Lance's assembled ceiling is BELOW Pyre's because (a) its own output is the lowest of any T3 fire card (2 burn) and (b) its scaling hook gates on an event almost nothing in the burn kit emits. It is a textbook under-tier T3 that will go unused.
finding.proposed_change: BUFF the T3 (never nerf Pyre). Make Quench Lance a real T3 burn detonator that self-fuels: replace the dead event_counter aura with effects = [{type:'damage', value:4, scale:{stat:'int',per:2,value:1}, condition:{enemy_has_stack:'burn', per_stack:true}}, {type:'dot', value:3, stack:'burn', scale:{stat:'int',per:2,value:1}}] (deal 4(int) per burn consumed via the Pyre path, then re-seed 3 burn). Keep cost 1s+1m and CD 2.4 unchanged (C1 — cost is the lever, tier != bigger cost). Sim of this exact rework restores parity-to-better vs Pyre: skeleton TTK 9000 (vs Pyre 8900), werewolf 10000 (vs 8900), depths_horror 7000 (vs 6750), and it is NOT oppressive (doom_knight cushion still only 0.174, TTK 21700). If the INT-scaling identity is to be preserved instead, at minimum lower the consume threshold (min_amount 6) AND fix the Pyre-path stack_consumed emission so the gate can actually trigger.

finding.title: Firestorm's peakOPS 0 is a single-card measurement artifact — in a real burn deck it OUT-performs the Flame Dart baseline; do NOT flag it as weak
finding.evidence: card-appearance.json lists t2-air-fire peakOPS 0 (looks dead). But its description 'For 4 seconds, if you play 3 or more cards, apply 10[burn]([int])' is a multi-card-conditional aura; isolated OPS can never satisfy 'play 3 cards in 4s', so 0 is an artifact. In-deck swap test (mage-offcurve-results.json, 6 repeats): swapping Firestorm INTO the lead slot (replacing Flame Dart) IMPROVED the deck — vs skeleton/werewolf/depths_horror TTK fell to 6900/6900/6900ms (baseline Flame Dart deck: 9000/10000/6383) and doom_knight cushion rose to 0.174 from baseline 0.091. nDecks 12, meanNormalWinRate 1.0, meanBossWinRate 0.875 — it is used and winning.
finding.diagnosis: Firestorm is a conditional burst aura that the isolated-OPS metric structurally cannot value (the gate needs other cards played). It is healthy in its native fast-cast burn shell. Flagging it as a 0-OPS dead card (as the raw appearance table invites) would be a false positive.
finding.proposed_change: No balance change. Methodology note: exclude multi-card-conditional auras (cards_played_within gates) from the 'low peakOPS = weak' screen and judge them only via in-deck swap tests. If any cosmetic improvement is wanted, raise the 1.8s cooldown toward the ~1.4s burn-aura norm (C1 CD lever) so it re-arms with the combo, but the sim shows this is not needed.

finding.title: Gale Echo's peakOPS 0 reflects 'deals no damage by design' (slow amplifier), not a broken T3 — it functions in a slow shell
finding.evidence: peakOPS 0 (card-appearance.json). Description 'For 12 seconds, every time you apply [slow], apply 1[slow]([int]). Haste 15% for 6s' — a pure slow-amplifier aura that deals zero damage alone, so 0 OPS is expected (C5: slow = PURE control, no damage). In-deck swap test: replacing the lead Sandfury (a 35-OPS slow DAMAGE card) with Gale Echo still wins all normals and actually closes a touch faster (skeleton TTK 16700 vs Sandfury-baseline 19100; werewolf 16700 vs 19100) and at higher cushion (skeleton 0.851 vs 0.802; doom_knight 0.339 vs 0.174) because the extra slow it stacks is converted to damage by Cinder Squall (2 dmg per slow). nDecks 9, winRate 1.0.
finding.diagnosis: Gale Echo is an intended no-damage control multiplier; OPS=0 is by design, not an off-curve weakness. Its real value only appears alongside a slow-payoff card (Cinder Squall / Sandfury), which a slow archetype always runs. Not a weak-card flag.
finding.proposed_change: No balance change required (it is a working C5-compliant control card). Methodology note: classify slow/stun amplifier auras as 'intended 0-OPS race-losers' alongside heal/armor, and validate them via swap tests, not OPS. Only consider a small CD cut (current 2.4s) if a future audit shows the slow archetype is too slow to assemble; the sim does not currently justify it.

finding.target: Heal-line magic cards: t2-air-water (Misting Veil, peakOPS 2.57), t3-air-air-water (Squall Aura, 4.5), t2-agility-water (Mist Step, 4.31), t2-defense-water (Vow of the Tide, 4.75), t3-earth-water-water (Brine Bedrock, 7.5), t3-earth-fire-water (Alchemic Drain, 8)
finding.title: Low-OPS heal/sustain magic cards are intended C3 race-losers, not off-curve-weak — they win with high cushion and are widely used; do NOT false-flag
finding.evidence: These cluster at the bottom of the T2/T3 OPS distribution (median T2 ~14.9, T3 ~12.3) purely because OPS measures damage and their value is HP/tempo: Misting Veil 'Heal 3([spi]). Haste 15%. Gain 1 mana. +1 int 6s', Squall Aura 'Heal 9([spi]). Haste 25%. Gain 4 mana', Alchemic Drain 'Apply 3 poison. Heal 6([spi])'. Their sim record is healthy: Misting Veil meanNormalWinRate 1.0 / cushion 0.929 (nDecks 11); Squall Aura 1.0 / 0.912; Alchemic Drain 1.0 / 0.913 (nDecks 39 — heavily played); Brine Bedrock 1.0 / 0.947 (nDecks 16). The heal-sustain mage archetype clears doom_knight at cushion 0.855 and every deeper boss at 0.92-0.98 (archetype-summary). static-confirmed already notes heal carries a hidden global +15%/SPI multiplier on top of the shown ([spi]) tag, so true heal output is UNDERSTATED by the card text, not overstated.
finding.diagnosis: C3 explicitly makes armor/heal intentionally race-losing (elite at survival to buy time for a second win condition, no heal->damage conversion). Their low OPS is the design, and the hidden SPI multiplier means they are stronger than their text/OPS implies. Flagging any of these as 'too weak / unused-tier' would be a false positive that violates C3.
finding.proposed_change: No balance change. Methodology note: bucket mana/SPI heal cards as intended race-losers (like armor) and evaluate them on cushion + archetype boss clears, never on OPS. The only genuine action here is the static-confirmed TEXT issue (surface or de-double-count the hidden SPI heal multiplier), which is a clarity fix, not a balance lever.

finding.target: t1-water (Mend, peakOPS 5.4, CD 2.0) vs t1-fire (Spark) / other T1 magic
finding.title: Mend's 2.0s cooldown is the slowest of any T1 card and out of step with the snappy-CD floor; a CD cut would rescue it without touching its value
finding.evidence: cards-index: t1-water Mend 'Heal 9.' cost 1m, cooldown 2.0 — the longest CD among T1s (Spark 1.2, Quake 1.4, Gust 1.2, Jab/Quickstep 1.2). Per C1 the CD floor is ~1.2s and CD should stay snappy; tier != longer CD. Mend still posts winRate 1.0 / cushion 0.886 because of the hidden SPI heal multiplier, so it is not failing — but as the starter heal it gates the heal archetype's early tempo behind a slow re-cast. The hidden global SPI multiplier (Heal 9 at SPI10 = 22) means raising the printed value is unnecessary; the lever is the CD.
finding.diagnosis: Mend is the clearest C1 mismatch in the T1 magic set: a clean single-effect T1 carrying a 2.0s cooldown when its peers sit at 1.2-1.4s. It is mild because the card still wins, but the long CD makes the entry-level heal feel worse than it scales.
finding.proposed_change: C1-compliant CD cut: lower Mend's cooldown from 2.0 to ~1.4s (matching Quake / the T1 magic norm) to restore tempo, leaving value (Heal 9 + hidden SPI mult) and cost (1m) unchanged. This rescues a slightly-off T1 by the cost/CD lever, not by inflating output. Low priority given the card already wins.

finding.target: tier-ops-perf.json (T2 vs T3 OPS distribution, 156 cards)
finding.title: T3 median OPS (15.33) is still BELOW T2 median (15.57) — the tier charter's 'T3 must out-ceiling T2' is violated at the central tendency
finding.evidence: tests/audit/val/analysis/tier-ops-perf.json: T1 median 6 (n7); T2 median 15.57, mean 19.62 (n36); T3 median 15.33, mean 23.26 (n120). T3 median is 0.24 BELOW T2 median. The T3 MEAN only looks higher because of long-tail outliers that static-confirmed.json proves are bugs, not power: Brine Crucible peakOPS 158.13 and Vein Splitter peakOPS 134.38 are both self-satisfied-gate cards (their 'if enemy has [stack]' bonus always fires), plus Razor Cadence 72.75. Strip those three and the T3 central tendency sits at/below T2. The charter (WORKFLOW-BRIEF.md tier charter) says T3's edge must be a higher CEILING when assembled (~+15-25%), never lower; the median says the typical T3 is no better than the typical T2.
finding.diagnosis: The OPS-bug outliers mask a real curve problem: most T3 cards do not out-ceiling the analogous T2 on the primary throughput axis. Because the inflated mean comes from confirmed self-gate bugs (which will be nerfed when fixed), the post-fix T3 mean will DROP, widening the gap. The honest measure (median) already shows T3 ≤ T2.
finding.proposed_change: Treat the T3 median lift as a tier-wide objective: after the per-card buffs below, re-run the OPS distribution and require T3 median >= T2 median * 1.10. Do NOT achieve this by nerfing T2 (C1/charter). Drive it by cutting cooldowns / removing exhaust on the under-curve T3s (C1: cost is the lever, cd stays snappy ~1.2-2.0s) and by raising per-stack/per-hit ceilings on build-around T3s. Note when interpreting the data that Brine Crucible/Vein Splitter OPS are bug-inflated and must not be used as the T3 'ceiling' benchmark.

finding.title: Supernova (T3 pure-fire) is weaker AND far more expensive than its T2 fire siblings — the pure-fire ladder goes DOWN at T3
finding.evidence: card-appearance.json: Supernova peakOPS 28.57, cost 2s+1m, cd 2.8, EXHAUST true, 17 decks, cushion 0.837, bossWR 0.867. Pyre (T2) peakOPS 36.43, cost 1m, cd 1.4, non-exhaust. Flame Dart (T2 burn) peakOPS 57.5, cost 1m, cd 1.2. So the pure-fire ladder is Spark T1 8.57 -> Pyre T2 36.43 -> Supernova T3 28.57 (DOWN). Supernova is simultaneously the most expensive card class (2s+1m) AND longest-cd (2.8) AND once-per-combat exhaust. Isolated carry sim (tests/audit/val/t3t2-iso-results.json): iso-pyre-T2 won at cushion 0.712 / TTK 11357ms; iso-supernova-T3-current won at cushion 0.545 / TTK 17213ms — the T3 carry is SLOWER and takes more damage than the T2 carry. In a dedicated burn-feed deck (tests/audit/val/t3t2-fix-results.json) fix-supernova-current only reached WR 0.800 (loses 1 in 5).
finding.diagnosis: The 5/burn-Pierce-to-all detonator has a high ceiling on paper, but exhaust caps it at a single cast and the 2s+1m / cd2.8 price makes that one cast unaffordable early, so it under-delivers vs a repeatable 1m Pyre. This is a pure tier inversion: the T3 loses to the T2 on cost, cooldown, OPS, and real-deck performance.
finding.proposed_change: BUFF the T3 (never nerf Pyre). Per C1, the primary fix is cost+cd: remove exhaust, drop cost from {stamina:2,mana:1} to {mana:1}, and cut cd 2.8 -> ~2.0. Sim-verified: tests/audit/val/t3t2-fix-results.json fix-supernova-buffA (no-exhaust, cd2.0, 1m) flips WR 0.800 -> 1.000 and lifts cushion 0.505 -> 0.648 without raising any value. To restore a clear T3>T2 ceiling, optionally also raise the detonator value from 5 to 6 per burn (effects index 1): fix-supernova-buffB hit cushion 0.693 / TTK 11353 (now matching/beating Pyre's carry profile) and is NOT oppressive (WR caps at 1.0 with a normal cushion). Keep it AoE Pierce so it stays the build-around fire payoff.

finding.title: Tempest Cadence (T3 pure-air) has less than HALF Tailwind's OPS and loses on every axis as a carry — over-costed/over-cd'd haste card
finding.evidence: card-appearance.json: Tempest Cadence peakOPS 24.17, cost 2m, cd 2.4, only 2 decks (nearly unused). Tailwind (T2) peakOPS 56.67, cost 1m, cd 1.2, 14 decks. T3 OPS is 43% of the T2. Isolated carry sim (tests/audit/val/t3t2-iso-results.json): iso-tailwind-T2 WR 0.600 / cushion 0.275 / dmg 142 / TTK 22783; iso-tempest-T3-current WR 0.600 / cushion 0.204 / dmg 123 / TTK 26767 — the T3 is worse on cushion, damage, and speed at equal win rate. The T3's 30%/10s haste cannot compound because at cd2.4 the card is rarely re-cast, whereas Tailwind's 20%/5s at cd1.2 loops tightly.
finding.diagnosis: Tempest's only edge over Tailwind is AoE + bigger single numbers, but the doubled cost (2m) and doubled cd (2.4) strangle the haste engine that makes the air element good, so it under-ceilings its own T2. Classic over-pricing on BOTH levers, which the charter says only genuinely elite cards may do.
finding.proposed_change: BUFF the T3 (never nerf Tailwind). Primary lever is cooldown (C1): cut cd 2.4 -> 1.6 so the 30% haste actually loops; cost cut 2m -> 1m is a secondary enabler. Sim-verified in a fuller air deck (tests/audit/val/tempest-results.json): cd1.6 lifts cushion 0.679 -> 0.707 and TTK 12743 -> 11885 (~7% faster); cost-cut on top adds nothing further (cd16 == cd16only), confirming cd is the binding constraint. In the thin carry deck (t3t2-fix-results.json) cost+cd together moved cushion 0.204 -> 0.267 and dmg 123 -> 130. Recommend cd 2.4 -> 1.6 as the core fix, optionally also cost 2m -> 1m; keep the AoE + 30%/10s haste so it remains the T3 air ceiling.

finding.title: Pure-water T3 Tidesong Aura OPS (9.0) sits below pure-water T2 Frostbind OPS (10.6) — but both are C3 race-losing support, so OPS is the wrong axis
finding.evidence: card-appearance.json: Tidesong Aura (T3) peakOPS 9.0, cost 2m, cd 2.4, 7 decks, cushion 0.946; Frostbind (T2) peakOPS 10.6, cost 1m, cd 1.4, 16 decks, cushion 0.926. cards-index: Tidesong is pure sustain (Heal 18([spi]), +2 SPI/combat, +3 mana — note the undocumented global SPI heal multiplier in static-confirmed.json makes its real heal higher than shown). Frostbind is stun+armor control. Both win-rate 1.0 / bossWR 1.0.
finding.diagnosis: This is a borderline case, not a clean violation: per C3 (armor/heal/stun are intentionally race-losing, no heal->damage conversion) neither water card is meant to compete on damage OPS, and Tidesong's job (sustain + ramp SPI + refund mana) is genuinely different from Frostbind's (control). The 'T3 must out-ceiling T2' charter is about damage ceiling; for these support cards the relevant axis is survivability, where Tidesong already edges Frostbind (cushion 0.946 vs 0.926). Flagging low so it is not mistaken for a damage inversion.
finding.proposed_change: No damage buff (would violate C3). If a tier feel-better is wanted, the C1-compliant lever is cd: cut Tidesong cd 2.4 -> ~1.8 so the heal/ramp cadence outpaces Frostbind's, keeping its support identity. Do NOT add a damage rider or armor->damage conversion. Treat as monitor-only unless the heal/sustain archetype shows weakness elsewhere.

finding.target: T3 pure-attack burn line (t3-attack-attack-fire Cinder Thrust, t3-fire-fire-fire Supernova) vs T2 t2-agility-fire (Flame Dart)
finding.title: No clean pure-burn T3 out-ceilings the best T2 burn (Flame Dart 57.5); the entire fire archetype's T3 ceiling is set by bug-inflated cards
finding.evidence: card-appearance.json burn cards: best T2 burn Flame Dart peakOPS 57.5 (1m/cd1.2). Highest pure-fire-attack T3s: Cinder Thrust 47.78 (2s/cd1.8), Supernova 28.57. Both BELOW Flame Dart. The only 'burn' T3 above 57.5 is Brine Crucible 158.13 — confirmed in static-confirmed.json as a bleed+burn self-gate bug (nDecks 1), not a clean fire payoff. Boss win rates also sag in the fire line: Supernova bossWR 0.867, Cinder Sprint 0.818, Cinderlance 0.875, Pyre Surge 0.909, Pyre 0.88 — burn underperforms vs bosses generally.
finding.diagnosis: Fire's T3 build-around ceiling is illusory: it reads high on mean only because of a self-satisfied-gate bug, while every honest pure-burn T3 is below the strongest T2 burn. So a player upgrading from Flame Dart into a fire T3 gets a worse repeatable throughput card, with extra cost/cd. This is the burn archetype's tier curve being inverted, on top of the Supernova-specific case above.
finding.proposed_change: After fixing the Brine Crucible / Vein Splitter / Steaming Plague self-gates (per static-confirmed.json, those are separate bug fixes), establish a true clean-fire T3 ceiling above Flame Dart's 57.5 by buffing the dedicated burn T3 throughput cards via C1 cooldown cuts: e.g. Cinder Thrust cd 1.8 -> 1.5 (raises its repeat OPS toward/above Flame Dart) and Supernova as in the dedicated finding. Verify post-buff that at least one honest pure-burn T3 has peakOPS > 57.5 in a burn-feed deck, without nerfing Flame Dart (C1/charter). Re-sim vs the boss battery since burn bossWR currently trails (0.82-0.91).
